Mature, but “naïve” T-cells emerge from the thymus and are not yet active
Mature, but “naïve” B-cells emerge from the Bone and are not yet active
“The central event in the generation of both humoral and cell mediated responses is the activation and clonal expansion of T cells”
Dendritic cells phagocytose antigen, display on MHX II, then migrate to secondary organs and display to Helper T-cells

Note: T-cells are enriched in the paracortex
T-Cell Receptor Complex:TCR-CD3

T-Cell activation
Costimulatory signals are required for full T-cell activation
Signal 1
Interaction of antigenic peptide with TCR-CD3 complex
(Interaction of CD4 or CD8 with MHC)
Signal 2
Interaction with CD28 on T cell and members of the B7 family on antigen presenting cell

Anergy
A state if immune unresponsiveness.  Induced when the T cell’s antigen receptor is stimulated, effectively freezing T cell responses pending a “second signal” from the antigen-presenting cell.  The delivery of the second signal by the APC cell rescues the activated T cell from anergy, allowing it to produce the lymphokines necessary for the growth of additional T cells.

“Once activated, the helper T cell can then activate a B cell that specifically displays the same complex of foreign peptide and class II MHC protein on its surface.”

“B-cells could be regarded as helping their helpers because the antigenic peptides produced within the endocytic processing pathway associate with class II MHC molecules and are presented on the B-cell membrane to the TH cell, inducing its activation.”
B-cell response to thymus-dependent antigens requires direct contact with TH cells, not simply exposure to TH derived cytokines.

“Whereas antigen-presenting cells such as dendritic cells and macrophages are omnivorous and ingest antigens nonspecifically, a B-cell generally presents only an antigen that it specifically recognizes.
